did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a distributed U-Shaped Split Federated Learning (USFL) method with a ResNet-18 deep learning model to perform image recognition tasks on the HAM10000 dataset, which consists of dermatoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel distributed U-Shaped Split Federated Learning (USFL) method, details its system architecture and algorithm (Section II and III), and presents experimental results evaluating its performance on the HAM10000 dataset using ResNet-18 (Section IV). It compares the proposed method against centralized, SL, FL, and SFL methods, which constitutes original experimental research rather than a literature review.

title: An Efficient and Privacy-Enhanced U-Shaped Split Federated Learning Method for Distributed Multi-Client Systems
journal: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Communications Workshops (ICC Workshops)
brief_summary: This study proposes a distributed U-Shaped Split Federated Learning (USFL) method to address privacy and efficiency challenges in image recognition tasks. The framework combines the parallel processing strengths of Federated Learning with the computational load distribution of Split Learning, utilizing a U-shaped architecture where lightweight layers remain on clients and complex layers are offloaded to edge servers. The authors evaluated the method using the HAM10000 dermatoscopic skin lesion dataset and the ResNet-18 model across various client and server configurations. Experimental results demonstrate that USFL achieves approximately 80% test accuracy, slightly outperforming centralized, FL, SL, and SFL baselines while offering superior stability and faster convergence. Additionally, the method significantly reduces communication overhead and training time compared to traditional FL and SFL approaches. The study concludes that USFL provides an effective, privacy-preserving, and efficient solution for distributed multi-client learning systems.

paper_terms: dermatoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions
lesion_types_studied.details: The study uses the HAM10000 dataset, which consists of dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The paper does not list specific sub-classifications (e.g., melanoma, nevus) but refers to the general category of pigmented skin lesions as the target for the image recognition task.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories of pigmented skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper performs image recognition on the HAM10000 dataset, which consists of dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The model generates predicted labels (Y_hat) and compares them with ground truth labels (Y) to compute accuracy and loss, indicating a classification task for identifying lesion classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, cited as reference [11] (Tschandl P, Rosendahl C, Kittler H. Scientific data, 2018).

use.training: The dataset was randomly partitioned into multiple shards and evenly distributed across clients (1-100 clients) for training the ResNet-18 model under an IID setting.
use.testing: Used to evaluate model accuracy, achieving approximately 80% test accuracy for the USFL method.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between USFL and baseline methods (Centralized, FL, SL, SFL) were based on observed metrics such as test accuracy, convergence speed, and stability without statistical significance testing.

USFL.notes: USFL achieved the highest accuracy and stability among compared methods. It showed faster convergence and smaller fluctuations compared to baselines.

Centralized.notes: USFL slightly outperformed the Centralized method.

FL.notes: Exhibited slower convergence and slightly lower final accuracy compared to USFL.

SL.notes: Exhibited slower convergence and slightly lower final accuracy compared to USFL.

SFL.notes: Achieved similar accuracy to USFL but with greater fluctuations.

HAM10000.notes: The study evaluated configurations with 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100 clients, alongside 1, 2, 5, and 10 edge servers. With 2 servers, USFL performed best with high stability. With 1 server, significant accuracy fluctuations and slower convergence were observed. With 5 and 10 servers, convergence speed was comparable to 2 servers, but accuracy slightly decreased and fluctuations increased due to higher communication overhead.

architecture_details.type: Distributed Split Learning combined with Federated Learning
architecture_details.structure: U-shaped architecture where data flows from client to server and back to client for label generation.
architecture_details.partitioning: Model partitioned into client-side network, server-side network, and client-back network.
architecture_details.client_layers: Layers 1-2 (lightweight, lower computational demand) deployed on the client.
architecture_details.server_layers: Layers 3-6 (computationally intensive) assigned to the edge server.
architecture_details.output_layer: Layer 7 (responsible for generating labels) remains on the client.
architecture_details.privacy_mechanism: Differential Privacy (DP) with Pixel-DP noise layer added to the client model output; gradient clipping and noise injection applied to intermediate parameters.
architecture_details.aggregation: FedAvg algorithm used by a cloud parameter server to aggregate weights from edge servers.

training_details.loss_function: Loss function computed by comparing predicted labels (Y_hat) with ground truth labels (Y) on the client.
training_details.training_schedule: Parallel execution across multiple clients; global model aggregation at the end of each epoch.

classification_or_output_head.details: Layer 7 on the client generates predicted labels for pigmented skin lesions.

preprocessing_techniques: Random partitioning of the HAM10000 dataset into multiple shards for IID distribution across clients
Noise injection (Pixel-DP) added to the output layer of the client model for privacy preservation
Gradient clipping (norm bound C') applied to intermediate parameters
conclusions: The proposed U-Shaped Split Federated Learning (USFL) method outperforms centralized, Split Learning (SL), Federated Learning (FL), and Split Federated Learning (SFL) methods in terms of model accuracy and runtime.
USFL achieves approximately 80% test accuracy on the HAM10000 dataset, slightly outperforming the Centralized method and exhibiting higher stability and faster convergence than FL and SL.
While SFL achieves similar accuracy to USFL, it exhibits greater accuracy fluctuations.
USFL demonstrates superior privacy protection compared to FL and higher accuracy than both FL and SL.
The optimal configuration for USFL involves using 2 edge servers, which yields the best performance with high stability and approximately 80% accuracy.
Using only 1 server leads to significant accuracy fluctuations and slower convergence due to computational bottlenecks.

Increasing the number of servers to 5 or 10 results in comparable convergence speed to 2 servers but slightly decreased accuracy and increased fluctuations due to higher communication and synchronization overhead.

As the number of clients increases (from 5 to 100), overall accuracy and stability gradually decrease, with significant performance degradation observed at 20, 50, and 100 clients due to increased communication overhead and gradient synchronization delays.
The best performance with minimal fluctuations is achieved with 5 and 10 clients.
The integration of differential privacy mechanisms and Pixel-DP noise injection enhances the robustness and security of the model without compromising its ability to learn.
The U-shaped split architecture effectively reduces the computational burden on clients and optimizes communication efficiency in distributed multi-client systems.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which consists of multi-source dermatoscopic images; no external validation on independent clinical datasets was performed, limiting generalizability.

The paper does not report specific class-wise performance metrics (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, or per-class accuracy for melanoma vs. benign lesions), only overall test accuracy (~80%), which may mask performance disparities across imbalanced classes.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was conducted to validate the significance of performance differences between USFL and baseline methods (Centralized, FL, SL, SFL).

The dataset was partitioned under an Independent and Identically Distributed (IID) assumption; the method's robustness to Non-IID data distributions, common in real-world clinical settings, was not evaluated.

The proposed USFL framework is a theoretical and simulation-based contribution focused on distributed systems architecture; it does not include a clinical deployment study or evaluation of real-world computational constraints on edge devices.

While Differential Privacy (Pixel-DP) is implemented, the specific privacy budget parameters (epsilon, delta) are discussed theoretically but not reported as fixed values used in the final experimental results, making reproducibility of the exact privacy-accuracy trade-off difficult.

The study evaluates scalability up to 100 clients but notes significant performance degradation (accuracy and stability drops) at higher client counts (20, 50, 100) due to communication overhead, suggesting limited practical utility for very large-scale deployments without further optimization.
